title: Annesley Stirling Memorial
crew_summary: 5 killed including pilot Sqn Ldr. Stephen Legh Cockbain, DFC
narrative: By January 1945, the Short Stirling was obsolete as a front-line bomber and was being withdrawn from service. Stirling EH988 of 1661 HCU at RAF Winthorpe was being ferried to Maghaberry in Northern Ireland for scrapping. Squadron Leader Cockbain, an experienced pilot who had been "rested" from operations and posted to Ferry Command, was at the controls. Shortly after takeoff, one of the starboard engines caught fire. Witnesses saw the aircraft begin to turn, the turn becoming increasingly steep. The Stirling entered cloud and went into an uncontrollable diving turn, crashing at Home Farm on the Annesley Park estate near Mansfield. All five crew members were killed. The crash was grimly ironic — these men died not on a bombing raid or training flight, but while delivering a worn-out aircraft to be broken up. The Stirling, with its relatively short wingspan and underpowered engines, was notoriously difficult to fly on asymmetric power when an engine failed — a fatal characteristic that claimed many crews.

crew.name: Stephen Legh Cockbain
crew.fate_detail: Killed when Stirling EH988 crashed at Home Farm, Annesley Park estate. Buried at Oxford (Botley) Cemetery.
crew.bio: Squadron Leader Cockbain held the Distinguished Flying Cross, indicating previous distinguished service on operations. He had been rested from front-line duties and was serving with Ferry Command at 1661 HCU, Winthorpe. He was 28 years old at the time of the crash.

crew.name: T.A. Ball
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the crash of Stirling EH988 at Annesley on 14 January 1945.

crew.name: E.C. Barton
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the crash of Stirling EH988 at Annesley on 14 January 1945.

crew.name: K. Harris
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the crash of Stirling EH988 at Annesley on 14 January 1945.

crew.name: J. Littlemore
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the crash of Stirling EH988 at Annesley on 14 January 1945.

cause_of_loss: Starboard engine fire; aircraft entered cloud in steep diving turn and crashed
memorial_details: A memorial garden was established at the Priory Hospital site (formerly Annesley Park) and dedicated on 16 September 1998, unveiled by Group Captain Cook with RAF veterans and surviving family members present. The memorial includes a stone of remembrance, a memorial bench, and an interpretive panel featuring a photograph of a Stirling bomber. The site is accessible to visitors at Priory Hospital.

external_narratives.summary: TracesOfWar documents the Annesley Stirling memorial near the crash site of EH988, noting it was erected in the late 1990s by the Stirling Memorial Group.
key_details: Memorial erected by Stirling Memorial Group
Located close to original crash site on Musters Estate

external_narratives.summary: RAF Commands archives hold the record of Stirling EH988 from 1661 HCU at Winthorpe, confirming it crashed on 14 January 1945 while being ferried to Northern Ireland for scrapping.
key_details: Aircraft was being ferried to Maghaberry for scrapping

external_narratives.summary: The Imperial War Museum's War Memorials Register lists the Crew of Stirling EH988 memorial, recording the five casualties: Ball, Barton, Cockbain, Harris, and Littlemore.
The IBCC Digital Archive records Squadron Leader Cockbain's loss in the Stirling EH988 crash, linking it to the 1661 Heavy Conversion Unit.
The Nottinghamshire County Council Roll of Honour records the Annesley 1945 Short Stirling Air Crash war memorial, noting the crash took place on the Annesley Park estate near Mansfield.
key_details: Crash at Home Farm, Annesley Park estate

external_narratives.summary: Local newspaper article recounting the Annesley crash, noting the aircraft attempted a forced landing at RAF Hucknall after losing two engines but crashed on a wooded ridge on the Musters Estate. Recovered aircraft fragments are held in a casket at Newark Air Museum.
key_details: Aircraft tried to reach RAF Hucknall
Crashed on wooded ridge on Musters Estate
Fragments stored at Newark Air Museum

local_contacts.role: Local authority responsible for memorial upkeep

sources: War Memorials Online ref 201089 (Historic England dataset) — 53.070801, -1.244604
location_confidence.verification_notes: Current coords (53.0711, -1.2422) are 160m east of actual location. Roadside/crash-site memorial: small garden of remembrance with rough-hewn stone, bench, interpretive panel ~50m SSW of A608/A611 roundabout, Mansfield Road, Annesley NG15 0AR. Formerly Musters Estate wooded ridge. Erected 1998 by Stirling Memorial Group. Two independent sources agree within 50m.
